Police Agency's specialist unit and highly trained police tactical unit conducting high-risk arrests and other dangerous law enforcement duties, the Thunder Squad.


Japan, which [Japan] continued on its [Japan’s] post-WW2 growth towards [Japan] becoming a political, economic, military and cultural superpower [Japan], [Japan] never experiencing “the Lost Decade” and or the Plaza Accords or any slump in growth and development in the late 1990s and 2010s, [Japan] Taiwan and the Pengu Islands as a province [of Japan] in a repeat of the return of Okinawa in 1972 to Japanese control sometime after the restoration of the monarchy [the Imperial House of Zhu and the Great Ming State] in Han China due to  the Han nationalist, Han supremacist, Chinese nationalist, Chinese supremacist Leninist-Stalinist Kuomingtang-ruled Nationalist China, which [Nationalist China] had [Nationalist China] previously [Nationalist China] been in exile on Taiwan and the Pengu Islands after [Nationalist China] losing control of mainland China to the CCP and what would become the PRC in the Chinese Civil War of 1947-1949, [Nationalist China] essentially being [Nationalist China] useless to both the USA and the USSR after the demise of the PRC and the subsequent return of the monarchy [the Imperial House of Zhu and the Great Ming State] in Han China, with Japan subsequently restoring the Japanese province on Formosa on Taiwan and the Pengu Islands after their [Taiwan and the Pengu Island's] return to Japanese control. 

In order to get money to prop up the failing Soviet economy and also as a unimplied non-aggression treaty, the Soviets also return Sahlakin and the Kurils to Japan, with the Japanese subsequently restoring the Japanese province of Karufuto on Sahlakin and the Kuril Islands. 

After pro-democracy, anti-military dictatorship protests in the joint US-PRC puppet state of the totalitarian military dictatorship of the Republic of Korea occupying South Korea spread to the Soviet satellite dictatorship of the Korean nationalist, Korean supremacist, Leninist-Stalinist Worker's Party of Korea-ruled totalitarian communist dictatorship of the Democratic People's Republic of Korea occupying North Korea, [the protests in the ROK spreading to the DPRK] toppling both post-WW2 Korean regimes [the DPRK and ROK], the Soviets and Americans jointly agree to return all of the Korean peninsula to Japan in a repeat of the Taiwanese and Okinawan situations.

After the return of the entire Korean peninsula to Japan, the Japanese restore the Japanese province of  Chōsen on the Korean peninsula.

Chapter VIII: The Dawn of a New Dynasty: The Restoration and Its Global Ramifications

In the spring of 1989, the echoes of dissent that had begun to sound within the squares of Beijing swiftly transformed into a cacophonous demand for change that resonated across the People's Republic of China (PRC). The anti-CCP protests, initially focused on ending corruption within the Chinese Communist Party (CCP) and advocating for democratic reforms, quickly burgeoned into a fervent movement that sought not only political but also far-reaching societal reform. It was a movement that, against all odds, would irrevocably alter the course of Chinese history and, by extension, the geopolitical landscape of the entire world.

The protests, drawing on a deep-seated heritage of revolutionary fervor, became a modern-day incarnation of the Xinhai Revolution. The CCP found itself in the role of the Qing dynasty, with the agitators casting themselves as the successors of the Tongmenghui revolutionaries. The collective desire for reform swiftly escalated into a clarion call for the outright abolition of the existing regime. As the movement gained momentum, it began to fracture the very foundations of the CCP's hold over China, leading to its eventual disintegration.

The aftermath of this second revolution was a fragmentation of the erstwhile monolithic PRC. Various regions, each with its unique cultural and historical identity, seized the opportunity to assert their autonomy. Guangdong, Guangxi, East Turkestan, Manchuria, Inner and Outer Mongolia, Tibet, Shanxi, and Yunnan each declared independence, setting the stage for a reconfiguration of China's political and territorial landscape.

Of particular significance was the restoration of the Empire of China, with the Imperial House of Zhu—at the instigation of the influential and previously unchecked Yellow Sand Society—being enthroned once more. This reborn empire sought to tether itself to the values and symbols of a bygone era, adopting the Beiyang Government's five-colored flag, albeit with a distinctly Ming symbol at its heart. The Empire also embraced the martial aesthetic of the Republic with its military insignia, while crafting a new national identity that revered both its historic legacy and the promises of a unified future.

The disintegration of the CCP's control allowed for the resurgence of various political entities and configurations. The remnant CCP was absorbed into the Young China Party (YCP), signaling a return to a holistic Chinese nationalism rooted in the three principles of Dr. Sun Yat-sen. This reconsolidated YCP, bearing the ideological remnants of the CCP, emerged as the ruling party of the reborn empire, striving towards a synthesis of traditional monarchic legitimacy and modern nationalist fervor.

Simultaneously, across the reconfigured territories of what was once the PRC, old kingdoms were resurrected, each advocating for a governance model that reflected their unique historical and cultural identities. Tibet saw the re-establishment of its theocratic Buddhist monarchy, while Yunnan revived the Dali Kingdom. There was even the curious emergence of a new Islamic sultanate in China's northwestern provinces, which sought to harken back to the Great Tang Empire—a reflection of China's diverse and multifaceted historical tapestry.

This geopolitical reshuffling did not occur in isolation. The dissolution of the PRC and the rise of the new Empire of China unveiled new diplomatic and strategic opportunities on the international stage. Japan, seizing on the shifting balance of power, moved to reassert its influence over Taiwan, the Pengu Islands, and, following a series of pro-democracy protests across Korea, the entire Korean Peninsula. This expansion of Japanese control was not merely an act of opportunistic imperialism but reflected a broader commitment to stabilizing the region and reasserting a sphere of influence that had historical precedence.

The world watched with bated breath as China, a nation with a civilization stretching back millennia, embarked on this bold experiment in governance. The restoration of the Empire under the House of Zhu was emblematic of a China that revered its past yet was unafraid to face the future. It was a testament to the resilience of a people who, after centuries of dynastic rule, colonial humiliation, and ideological strife, sought to reclaim their destiny on their terms.

As the 21st century dawned, the global order found itself at a crossroads, with the emergence of this new Chinese Empire challenging established norms and forging new alliances. The echoes of the 1989 protests had indeed given rise to a new era, one marked by a rekindled sense of Chinese identity and aspiration, fundamentally altering not just the nation’s trajectory but also the very fabric of international relations.

Staying and continuing with the alternate history scenario prompted and generated in the previous entry, remembering to stick to the history book chapter format: How did the 15 April 1989 – 4 June 1989 Tiananmen Square anti-CCP protests end up escalating into a repeat of the Xinhai Revolution and why were the MSS, PLA, PAP and People's Police unable to prevent this [the 15 April 1989 – 4 June 1989 Tiananmen Square anti-CCP protests becoming |the 15 April 1989 – 4 June 1989 Tiananmen Square anti-CCP protests | a repeat |the 15 April 1989 – 4 June 1989 Tiananmen Square anti-CCP protests| of the Xinhai Revolution?

Chapter IX: Escalation Towards Revolution: The 1989 Protests and Systemic Breakdown

The dramatic transformation of the 1989 protests into a wholesale revolution, mirroring the Xinhai Revolution of 1911-1912, was the culmination of a complex interplay of political dissent, systemic failures, and an underestimation of public sentiment by the Chinese government and its security apparatus. This chapter seeks to unravel the mechanisms that led to the incapacity of the Ministry of State Security (MSS), People's Liberation Army (PLA), People's Armed Police (PAP), and the People's Police to quell what initially presented as a series of peaceful demonstrations advocating for anti-corruption measures and democratic reforms.

### The Seeds of Dissent

The protests that filled Tiananmen Square in the spring of 1989 were not spontaneous eruptions of public discontent but rather the climax of years of increasing frustration with the Chinese Communist Party (CCP)'s policies, which were seen as veering increasingly towards corruption, elitism, and detachment from the populace. Economic reforms had widened the inequality gap, while political freedoms remained tightly curtailed. The tragic passing of Hu Yaobang, a reformist figure within the CCP, acted as a catalyst, converting simmering discontent into overt protest.

### The Failure of the Security Apparatus

**1. Underestimation of the Protests:** The Chinese government, and by extension its security forces, gravely underestimated the depth and breadth of the popular dissatisfaction. Initial responses to the protests were marked by a degree of leniency, predicated on the belief that the demonstrations would fizzle out as had previous instances of public dissent. This misjudgment provided protestors with the critical window needed to organize and galvanize broader support.

**2. Fractured Command and Control:** The leadership crisis within the CCP, exacerbated by Deng Xiaoping's semi-retirement and the lack of a clear succession plan, resulted in a fragmented command structure. This indecisiveness permeated down to the security forces, leading to inconsistent responses to the protests. As a result, by the time a coherent strategy was formulated, the protest movement had already gained a momentum that was difficult to contain.

**3. Lack of Effective Countermeasures:** The security apparatus—comprising the MSS, PLA, PAP, and the People's Police—was trained and equipped for conventional threats and was ill-prepared to handle widespread civil disobedience. Their heavy-handed tactics, designed to intimidate through a display of force, instead galvanized public opinion against the government, nationally and internationally.

**4. The Role of the Military:** The PLA's role in the events was particularly complex. As a force priding itself on being the "people's army," many units displayed reluctance to act against the protestors. There were reports of fraternization between soldiers and demonstrators, undermining attempts to restore order. The drawn-out nature of the protests further strained military logistics and morale, creating fissures within the institution.

### The Spiral Into Revolution

As the protests spread from Tiananmen Square to cities and provinces across China, they began to adopt increasingly revolutionary rhetoric. What had started as calls for reform evolved into demands for the outright dismantling of the CCP's monopoly on power. The government's inability to suppress the demonstrations emboldened the movement, drawing parallels with the successful overthrow of the Qing dynasty.

The pivotal turning point was a series of mass defections from within the CCP, the military, and other state organs, as personnel chose to side with what they perceived as an inevitable tide of change. This phenomenon mimicked the crumbling of military loyalty that precipitated the fall of the Qing. Additionally, the international spotlight on the protests, magnified by emerging global media technologies, exacted diplomatic pressure that further isolated the CCP regime.

### Conclusion

In retrospect, the transformation of the Tiananmen Square protests into a full-scale revolution was facilitated by a confluence of historical grievances, systemic weaknesses within the state security apparatus, and a decisive shift in public sentiment. The failure of the MSS, PLA, PAP, and People's Police to prevent the repeat of the Xinhai Revolution was emblematic of a broader crisis within the CCP's governance model. The inability to either pre-emptively address the root causes of discontent or effectively manage the crisis once it unfolded, ultimately sealed the fate of the CCP's rule, paving the way for the reconfiguration of China's political landscape in the aftermath.

Staying and continuing with the alternate history scenario prompted and generated in the previous entry, remembering to stick to the history book chapter format: How did the second Xinhai Revolution sparked by 15 April 1989 – 4 June 1989 Tiananmen Square anti-CCP protests end up leading to the disintegration of the CCP's control over China and the subsequent end of the PRC, with Guangdong, Guangxi, East Turkestan, Manchuria, Inner and Outer Mongolia, Tibet, Shanxi and Yunnan all [Guangdong, Guangxi, East Turkestan, Manchuria, Inner and Outer Mongolia, Tibet, Shanxi and Yunnan] subsequently [Guangdong, Guangxi, East Turkestan, Manchuria, Inner and Outer Mongolia, Tibet, Shanxi and Yunnan] breaking away [Guangdong, Guangxi, East Turkestan, Manchuria, Inner and Outer Mongolia, Tibet, Shanxi and Yunnan] from Han China after the fall of the CCP and PRC to the Second Xinhai Revolution and what was the international response to this [Guangdong, Guangxi, East Turkestan, Manchuria, Inner and Outer Mongolia, Tibet, Shanxi and Yunnan breaking away from Han China as a result of the fall of the CCP and PRC to the second Xinhai Revolution caused by the 15 April 1989 – 4 June 1989 Tiananmen Square anti-CCP protests]?

Chapter X: Fragmentation and Rebirth: The Disintegration of the People’s Republic and the International Aftermath

### The Crumbling Edifice

The second Xinhai Revolution, as the 1989 protests came to be known, set off a chain of events that ultimately led to the unraveling of the Chinese Communist Party's (CCP) stronghold over China. The initial calls for anti-corruption, democratic reforms, and greater freedoms rapidly morphed into a nationwide movement that questioned the very legitimacy of the CCP's rule. As the protests swelled, they exposed and widened cracks within the CCP and its apparatus, precipitating a catastrophic loss of control that reverberated across the entire country.

### Breakaway Regions

The climax of the revolution saw an unprecedented decentralization of power. As central authority weakened, regional leaders and movements, driven by long-suppressed ethnic, cultural, and political aspirations, seized the moment to assert their autonomy. 

**Guangdong and Guangxi**, with their unique linguistic and cultural identity, spearheaded the move towards regional autonomy, rallying around local leadership that promised a closer alignment with their aspirations and interests.

**East Turkestan** leveraged the revolution to amplify its longstanding quest for independence, galvanizing the support of its predominantly Muslim population against the Han Chinese rule, citing longstanding grievances and aspirations for self-determination.

**Manchuria** saw an opportunity to resurrect its historical identity, distinct from the Han Chinese mainstream, fueled by economic aspirations and a desire to renegotiate its place within a broader Chinese federation.

**Inner and Outer Mongolia** echoed these sentiments of independence, driven by a revival of Mongolian nationalism and a desire to reunify with the independent nation of Mongolia, seeking to restore a sense of historical continuity and cultural autonomy.

**Tibet**’s breakaway was underpinned by the quest for religious and cultural freedom, rallying around the Dalai Lama's leadership, who had long symbolized Tibetan resistance against Chinese domination.

**Shanxi and Yunnan** each pursued autonomy driven by unique regional identities, histories, and the strategic imperative to control local resources and governance.

These movements did not face the unified, forceful resistance that might have been expected from the CCP due to its preoccupation with maintaining control over the core Han regions and the capital. The military, fractured and demoralized, was unable to mount an effective response against the wave of secession.

### The International Response

The international community watched these developments with a mixture of apprehension and opportunism. The weakening of a unified China presented both risks to global stability and opportunities to redefine geopolitical alignments.

**The United States and European Nations** initially responded with cautious optimism. They extended diplomatic recognition to the breakaway regions that demonstrated a commitment to democratic governance, hoping to foster stability through diplomatic and economic support.

**Japan**, with its historical interests in the region, navigated the situation with strategic prudence, establishing relations with the new entities, particularly Manchuria and the Korean peninsula, to ensure a balance of power that favored its regional security interests.

**Russia** viewed the disintegration with a complex mix of opportunity and concern. On one hand, it opened avenues for increased influence in Mongolia and Central Asia. On the other hand, the fragmentation threatened to destabilize its borders and invited a reevaluation of its strategic posture in Asia.

**India**, sharing a contentious border with Tibet, welcomed the region's independence, bolstering its stance against Chinese territorial claims. It sought to strengthen ties with Tibet and the new autonomous regions to form a counterbalance against Han China.

**The United Nations**, amidst these shifting sands, played a mediating role, advocating for peaceful transitions and the preservation of human rights, while grappling with the challenge of recognizing new states amid China's reconstruction.

### Conclusion

The aftermath of the 1989 protests and the subsequent revolution was a China vastly different from before. The dissolution of the CCP's grip ushered in an era of fragmentation, where regions, once peripheral to Beijing's power, emerged as autonomous entities, each charting its course. The international community, while initially tentative, gradually aligned with the new geopolitical reality, recognizing and engaging with the new political entities that emerged from the ashes of the People’s Republic. 

This period of transformation marked the end of an era of centralized authoritarian rule in China and the beginning of a complex, multipolar landscape in East Asia, with implications that would resonate well into the 21st century. The rebirth of China into a constellation of independent states and a reconstituted Han core under the revived Empire symbolized not just the resilience and diversity of China's regional identities but also the enduring aspiration for self-determination and governance that reflects the will of the people.

Staying and continuing with the alternate history scenario prompted and generated in the previous entry, remembering to stick to the history book chapter format: How did the influence of the Yellow Sand Society spread in post-CCP, post-PRC Han China after the second Xinhai Revolution sparked by 15 April 1989 – 4 June 1989 Tiananmen Square anti-CCP protests and the subsequent disintegration of the CCP's control over China and the subsequent end of the PRC, with Guangdong, Guangxi, East Turkestan, Manchuria, Inner and Outer Mongolia, Tibet, Shanxi and Yunnan all [Guangdong, Guangxi, East Turkestan, Manchuria, Inner and Outer Mongolia, Tibet, Shanxi and Yunnan] subsequently [Guangdong, Guangxi, East Turkestan, Manchuria, Inner and Outer Mongolia, Tibet, Shanxi and Yunnan] breaking away [Guangdong, Guangxi, East Turkestan, Manchuria, Inner and Outer Mongolia, Tibet, Shanxi and Yunnan] from Han China after the fall of the CCP and PRC to the Second Xinhai Revolution, and why was the last truly Han Chinese monarchy [the Imperial House of Zhu and the Great Ming State] chosen as the government of independent post-CCP, post-PRC China? Did this have to do with the first Xinhai Revolution's goal of "Overthrowing Qing, restoring Ming"?

Chapter XI: The Crescent of Change: The Resurgence of the Yellow Sand Society and the Restoration of the Ming Dynasty

In the sweeping tide of transformation that followed the second Xinhai Revolution and the disintegration of the Chinese Communist Party's (CCP) dominance, a deeply rooted entity emerged from the shadows to cast a long influence over the future of Han China: the Yellow Sand Society. This chapter delves into the resurgence of this millennia-old folk religious sect, its role in shaping post-CCP Han China, and the consequent restoration of the Imperial House of Zhu as the governing dynasty. 

### The Yellow Sand Society's Ascendance

Post-revolution, amidst the tumult of redefining national identity and governance, the Yellow Sand Society, which had long operated at the margins of legality under the CCP, found new relevance. Its ideology, a potent blend of militant self-defense, tax resistance, romantic monarchism, and a fervent belief in the restoration of the Imperial House of Zhu (Ming Dynasty), resonated with a populace weary of authoritarian rule and yearning for a return to cultural and historical roots.

The society's survival through decades of suppression was a testament to its deep entrenchment in the cultural and spiritual psyche of the Han Chinese people. Its members, drawn from varied backgrounds including scholars, peasants, and even former CCP officials disillusioned with the regime, became the vanguard of a movement seeking to realign China's future with its imperial past.

### The Strategy of Influence

The Yellow Sand Society’s spread in post-CCP Han China was neither accidental nor merely the result of ideological appeal. The society had systematically infiltrated key societal nodes during the CCP's rule, biding its time while accruing influence within local communities, temples, and, crucially, within ranks of the disaffected military. As the CCP's control waned, the society's covert networks activated, swiftly moving to occupy the vacuum of power and influence. They effectively mobilized the populace around a unifying narrative of national rebirth, leveraging historical grievances against the Manchu Qing dynasty, and fostering a yearning for the restoration of the last Han Chinese dynasty—the Ming.

### Overthrowing Qing, Restoring Ming

The choice of the Imperial House of Zhu as the emblem of a rejuvenated Han China was deeply symbolic, echoing the slogan of the first Xinhai Revolution: "Overthrowing the Qing, restoring the Ming." This slogan encapsulated not just a political objective but a cultural and spiritual renaissance aimed at reconnecting with a golden age before the perceived humiliations and disruptions of foreign Manchu rule and subsequent CCP governance. Within this context, the Yellow Sand Society's vision aligned perfectly with the nation's mood of introspection and longing for a glorious past. They adeptly framed the restoration of the Ming Dynasty not merely as a return to monarchism but as a correction of historical wrongs and a rebirth of Chinese civilization.

### Crafting the New Old Order

The restored Ming Dynasty, while embodying historical continuity, was not a mere revival of ancient rites and structures. The Yellow Sand Society, along with its broad coalition of supporters, understood the need for a model of governance that accommodated modern political, economic, and social realities. Thus, the new Ming regime was characterized by a blend of traditional monarchic legitimacy and contemporary statecraft—a hybrid that sought to harness China's rich past to navigate its future.

In shaping this new old order, the society played a pivotal role in advising on constitutional structures, incorporating elements of democratic representation, and advocating for policies that emphasized social welfare, national rejuvenation, and economic revitalization. Their influence ensured that the restored Ming governance model was not anachronistic but a forward-looking embodiment of centuries-old aspirations infused with modern governance principles.

### Conclusion

In conclusion, the ascendancy of the Yellow Sand Society and the consequent restoration of the Imperial House of Zhu as the guiding force of post-CCP, post-PRC Han China was a multifaceted phenomenon. It was rooted in a deep-seated cultural and spiritual yearning for a return to perceived historical greatness, catalyzed by contemporary disillusionment with authoritarian rule. The society's strategic acumen in mobilizing this sentiment paved the way for a unique experiment in national reconstruction, blending the ancient and the modern. This chapter has traced the contours of this transformation, highlighting the society's role in shaping the vision and structures of the reborn Ming state, which stood as a testament to China's enduring legacy and its capacity for renewal amidst the ashes of its recent past.

Chapter XII: The Beiyang Precursor: Foundations of the Restored Monarchy

In a momentous epoch marked by upheavals and radical shifts within the fabric of Chinese society, the restoration of the Chinese monarchy under the aegis of the Imperial House of Zhu was an event of profound historical and symbolic significance. The conduit for this restoration—the Empire of China as initially established by Yuan Shikai during his brief tenure as emperor—serves as a pivotal context in understanding the form and substance of the restored Ming State. This chapter aims to elucidate why the structure and legacy of the Beiyang Government-ruled First Republic of China and its transformation into the short-lived Empire of China under Yuan Shikai provided the template for the reestablishment of the Imperial House of Zhu.

### The Historical Context of Yuan Shikai's Empire

Yuan Shikai's attempt in 1915 to establish the Empire of China was seen, in retrospect, as an abortive effort to meld the concepts of monarchy and modern statecraft at a time of great national disarray. Yuan's vision was rooted in the belief that a strong, centralized authority in the form of a monarchy could provide the stability and continuity China needed to navigate its internal turmoil and external pressures. However, his reign was short-lived, succumbing to widespread opposition and his subsequent death in 1916, leading to a period of fragmentation and warlordism.

### The Legacy and Lessons

Despite its brevity, Yuan Shikai’s empire left an indelible mark on the collective consciousness of the Chinese polity. It symbolized a critical juncture between the ancient imperial order and the nascent republicanism, illuminating the complexities of transitioning from millennia of dynastic rule toward a modern state. The failure of Yuan's empire was not merely political but also ideological, revealing the challenges of imposing a monarchical system atop a society undergoing profound transformation.

### The Choice of Restoration through Yuan's Model

The decision to channel the restoration of the Ming dynasty through the conceptual framework of Yuan Shikai's Empire of China was influenced by several key considerations:

1. **Historical Continuity and Legitimacy**: Yuan's model provided a historical parallel for transitioning from a republic back to a monarchy, which resonated with the proponents of monarchical restoration. It suggested that such a shift was not antithetical to the progression of Chinese history but rather a return to its traditional roots.

2. **Centralization and Modernization**: The model offered a blueprint for centralizing power while adopting elements of modern governance. Yuan's empire, despite its flaws, was an experiment in modernizing the state apparatus, military, and economy, which were deemed crucial for the rejuvenation of China under the restored Ming.

3. **Navigating Republican Sentiments**: The early 20th-century republicanism and the late 20th-century democratic aspirations could not be wholly disregarded. By invoking Yuan's Empire—initially born out of the Republic—as a transitional phase towards the Ming restoration, the movement managed to straddle traditional monarchism and contemporary political ideologies, offering a compromised path that acknowledged the republic's role in China's modern history.

4. **Lessons from Yuan’s Failures**: Yuan Shikai's missteps provided invaluable lessons in the dangers of autocratic overreach and the necessity of garnering broad-based support. The restoration efforts consciously sought to avoid repeating these errors by emphasizing the Ming monarchy’s benevolent and enlightened rule, contrasting it with Yuan’s hubris and despotism.

### Implementing the Vision

The restoration blueprint, thus inspired by the Beiyang paradigm, sought to strike a balance between monarchical authority and the participation of modern political structures such as a constitution, a legislative body, and a defined legal system. This approach was envisioned as a means to both anchor the new state in the revered traditions of the Ming and to propel it into the future with a governance model responsive to the needs and challenges of the 21st century.

### Conclusion

The choice of Yuan Shikai's Empire of China as the template for the restored Ming monarchy was a strategic maneuver that leveraged the lessons of history to forge a vision of monarchy compatible with contemporary politico-social expectations. It exemplified a nuanced understanding of the past, not as a relic to be mourned but as a wellspring of wisdom for shaping the future. In this reconstructed Empire of China, the Imperial House of Zhu found a vessel suited to carry forward the legacy of the Ming while navigating the uncharted waters of modern statecraft, embodying the aspirations of a nation poised between reverence for its imperial past and the imperatives of modern governance.

And how was the first Republic of China's and the Empire of China's shared National Assembly restored through the highest organ of state power of the People’s Republic of China and also the only branch of government in China with all state organs from the State Council to the Supreme People’s Court (SPC) being subservient to it per the principle of unified power, the National People’s Congress with the CCP subsequently being [the CCP] reabsorbed [the CCP] into the YCP soon after, with the YCP replacing the CCP as the dominant political organization [the YCP] in China?

Chapter XIII: Continuity Amidst Change: The Paradox of the "People's Republic of China" and the Restoration of the National Assembly

The restoration of the Imperial House of Zhu and the subsequent establishment of a monarchy modeled after Yuan Shikai's brief empire posed a unique conundrum for the architects of post-revolutionary China. They were tasked with reconciling the imperial legacy with the realities of a nation that had undergone significant political, social, and ideological transformations under both the Republic and the People's Republic. A critical aspect of this intricate balancing act was the decision to retain the name "People's Republic of China" (PRC) for the restored state and the reconstitution of the National Assembly as the pinnacle of its governance structure, absorbing the roles and responsibilities of the National People's Congress.

### Maintaining the Name "People's Republic of China"

The decision to maintain the name "People's Republic of China" in the international arena, despite the restoration of the monarchy, was multifaceted, rooted in both practical and symbolic considerations:

1. **International Recognition and Continuity**: In the volatile geopolitical landscape of the post-revolution era, preserving the name "People's Republic of China" ensured a degree of continuity in international relations. It sidestepped the potentially fraught process of renegotiating diplomatic recognitions, treaties, and memberships in international bodies under a new state nomenclature.

2. **Legitimacy and Sovereignty**: The name, having been recognized globally for decades, carried with it an assertion of sovereignty over the same territorial expanse claimed by the PRC before the revolution. It was a declaration that, while the form of government had transformed, the nation’s continuity and its claim to its territories remained unchanged.

3. **Symbolic Concession to Republican Ideals**: Retaining the "People's Republic" designation also represented a symbolic nod to the republican principles that had influenced China's political discourse since the early 20th century. It acknowledged the complex socio-political evolution China had undergone, suggesting that the new monarchy would not entirely repudiate the legacy of the Republic or the aspirations of its people for representation and participation in governance.

### Restoration of the National Assembly

The reconstitution of the National Assembly as the supreme organ of state power, absorbing the functionalities of the National People's Congress, was a strategic move aimed at melding monarchic governance with elements of representative democracy. This reinstitution mirrored the institutional framework of the early Republic and Yuan Shikai's empire, serving several key purposes:

1. **Institutionalizing a Monarchy-Democracy Hybrids**: The restored National Assembly was conceptualized as a mechanism to ensure that the monarchy operated within a framework of checks and balances. It aimed to incorporate democratic principles into the governance model, allowing for legislative oversight and public representation in law-making, even as the monarchy set the broader policy directions.

2. **Unifying Political and Legal Frameworks**: The National Assembly, by amalgamating the roles of historical legislative bodies and the National People's Congress, became the linchpin for unifying China’s political and legal systems post-restoration. This unified structure facilitated streamlined governance and law-making, essential for the stability and efficacy of the new regime.

3. **Absorption of the CCP into the YCP**: The reintegration of the CCP into the more broad-based Young China Party (YCP) marked a significant realignment of China’s political landscape. This merger effectively consolidated the revolutionary and nationalist elements within the country’s political elite, channeling their energies into the project of national rejuvenation under the restored monarchy. Within this context, the National Assembly played a crucial role in legitimizing and moderating the influence of different political factions, ensuring that the transition to a new political order did not devolve into factionalism.

### Conclusion

The paradoxical decision to retain the name "People's Republic of China" and the strategic restoration of the National Assembly under the restored monarchy were emblematic of the new regime's efforts to navigate the complexities of China's historical legacy, political evolution, and the aspirations of its populace. By positioning the state as a continuation rather than a repudiation of the PRC, the architects of the restoration sought to signal both domestically and internationally that the new monarchy would be a modern, inclusive, and progressive regime. This approach aimed to leverage the symbolic capital of the Republic and the People's Republic to bolster the legitimacy and stability of the restored Ming monarchy, showcasing an innovative melding of China’s rich historical traditions with the imperatives of contemporary governance.

Chapter XIV: Reunification and Relinquishment: The Nationalist Retreat and the Return to Japanese Sovereignty

The period following the tumultuous collapse of the People’s Republic of China (PRC) and the re-establishment of a monarchical order under the Imperial House of Zhu heralded a new era in Chinese politics and its territorial configuration. This chapter delves into the complex motivations behind the Kuomintang (KMT)-ruled Nationalist Chinese government in exile's decision to agree to reunification with mainland China and the unexpected cession of Taiwan and the Pengu Islands back to Japanese control.

### Nationalist China’s Decision for Reunification

The Nationalist government, having maintained its base in Taiwan since its retreat in 1949, found itself in an increasingly precarious position by the late 20th century. The global geopolitical landscape and the domestic political climate within Taiwan were shifting, challenging the feasibility of its claim over mainland China. Several factors influenced the Nationalist decision to reunite with the mainland:

1. **Ideological Resonance with the Restored Monarchy**: The restoration of the monarchy under the principles of the Beiyang Government-ruled First Republic of China as restructured by Yuan Shikai, albeit a monarchy, evoked a sense of nationalist fervor and historical continuity that resonated with the KMT. The restoration’s emphasis on Chinese sovereignty, territorial integrity, and national rejuvenation aligned with the core tenets of Sun Yat-sen’s Three Principles of the People, which the KMT had long championed.

2. **Pragmatic Acknowledgment of Changed Realities**: The fragmentation of the PRC and the establishment of numerous autonomous territories underscored a new political reality—that the centralized authority over all historic Chinese territories could not be feasibly claimed by any single entity, including the KMT. Reunification offered a pathway to participate in, and shape, the newly ordered mainland while preserving the core of Chinese civilization.

3. **Pressure and Incentivization from International Actors**: The KMT's decision was also influenced by diplomatic pressures and incentives from global powers interested in stabilizing East Asia. These actors, including the United States and Japan, offered economic aid and security assurances that sweetened the prospects of reunification and made the relinquishment of certain territories more palatable.

### The Return of Taiwan and the Pengu Islands to Japanese Sovereignty

The cession of Taiwan and the Pengu Islands back to Japan was perhaps the most surprising development in the post-revolutionary reconfiguration of East Asia. The reasons for this dramatic shift were multifold:

1. **Strategic Concessions for Greater Stability**: In the negotiations surrounding reunification, the return of these territories to Japanese control was seen as a strategic concession that would ensure greater regional stability and balance. Moreover, Japan’s economic and technological leadership in Asia positioned it as a crucial partner for the newly configured Chinese state in its modernization efforts.

2. **Recognition of Changed Political Realities**: The Nationalist government recognized that Taiwan's demographic, economic, and political landscapes had evolved significantly since 1949. The island had developed a distinct identity and a functioning democracy that made the prospect of integrating it into a monarchical system under mainland control untenable.

3. **Economic and Security Guarantees**: Japan, in return for regaining sovereignty over Taiwan and the Pengu Islands, pledged substantial economic investments and technological partnerships not only with these territories but also with the newly unified mainland. These guarantees were critical for the KMT and the newly restored monarchy to secure the foundations of their rule and ensure the economic viability of reunified China.

### Conclusion

The reunification of Nationalist China with the Han-controlled mainland and the subsequent transfer of Taiwan and the Pengu Islands to Japanese sovereignty were landmark events in the aftermath of the second Xinhai Revolution. These decisions were emblematic of the profound shifts in political, territorial, and ideological alignments that marked the era. For the KMT, reunification under the restored monarchy represented a concession to the inexorable tides of change, coupled with an opportunity to realign its historical mission with the evolving contours of Chinese statehood. For Taiwan and the Pengu Islands, the transition to Japanese control opened a new chapter, one that promised continuity of their democratic institutions and integration into a rapidly modernizing East Asian sphere under Japanese aegis.

Chapter XV: Symbols of Renewal: Constructing the Identity of the Restored Empire

Following the reunification of the Nationalist government in exile with mainland China and the establishment of the restored Empire of China under the Zhu dynasty, a comprehensive effort was made to define and institutionalize the symbols that would represent the reborn Chinese state. This chapter explores the deliberative process behind the selection of the national flags, naval jacks, the national flower, the imperial seal, the national emblem, and the choice of the national anthem for the Zhu-ruled restored Empire of China.

### Selection of the National Flags and Naval Jacks

The national flags and naval jacks of the restored Empire of China were the result of an extensive process that sought to merge historical continuity with the ideological underpinnings of the new regime. The main national flag reintroduced the historical Beiyang Government's Five-Colored Flag but incorporated the Ming Sun and Moon symbol in the center. This design was chosen for several reasons:

1. **Historical Continuity**: The five-colored flag was a potent symbol of the early Republic and represented the unity of China's major ethnic groups. Its adaptation reaffirmed the restored Empire's commitment to national unity and ethnic inclusiveness.
   
2. **Symbolic Integration**: The inclusion of the Ming Sun and Moon symbol asserted the legitimacy of the Zhu dynasty's rule and the restoration of the Ming imperial lineage, imbuing the flag with a sense of historical destiny and revival.

For naval jacks, the decision was made to adapt the KMT's Blue Sky, White Sun, and a Wholly Red Earth flag, substituting the "white sun" with a yellow sun featuring the Chinese character for "Ming." This modification served multiple symbolic purposes:

1. **Naval Heritage**: It acknowledged the historical significance of the KMT's contributions to modernizing China's naval forces and their role in defending Chinese sovereignty.
   
2. **Imperial Tribute**: Replacing the sun symbol with one directly referencing the Ming dynasty paid homage to China's maritime explorations during the Ming era, underlining the new regime's aspirations for a revitalized global presence.

### National Flower and Imperial Seal

The selection of the peony as the national flower and the creation of a new imperial seal encapsulated both the cultural values and the aesthetic sensibilities of the restored Empire. The peony, known for its beauty and resilience, symbolized prosperity and peace—a fitting emblem for a nation on the path to rejuvenation.

The imperial seal was designed to blend traditional motifs with symbols of the Zhu dynasty, incorporating elements that signified the unity and longevity of the Chinese state under the auspices of the Ming restoration. This seal would come to signify the authority and legitimacy of the imperial family in state matters.

### National Emblem and the Decision on the National Anthem

The national emblem adopted motifs from the Beiyang Government's Twelve Symbols National Emblem but introduced modernistic elements that reflected the restored Empire's forward-looking vision. Behind the main insignia, two crossed flags representing the republic's military valor during the Xinhai Revolution were added, merging the martial heritage of the Republic with the dynastic traditions of the Empire.

The reinstatement of the anthem "China heroically stands in the Universe" as the national anthem was symbolically significant. Originally associated with nationalist fervor and the unification of China, its re-adoption served several key purposes:

1. **Continuity and Unity**: It evoked the struggles and triumphs of China's path to modern nationhood, serving as a unifying call that transcended dynastic and republican divisions.
   
2. **Ideological Reconciliation**: By embracing an anthem from a pivotal period in China's modern history, the restored Empire signaled its intent to reconcile the republican legacy with the monarchical present, embodying a synthesis of China's diverse paths to national rejuvenation.

### Conclusion

The meticulous crafting of the symbols of the restored Empire of China was a project aimed at constructing a cohesive national identity that reconciled the myriad threads of China's historical experience. The choices made in terms of flags, emblems, and anthems were not merely aesthetic but deeply ideological, aimed at fostering a sense of unity and purpose among the Chinese people. Through these symbols, the restored Empire sought to articulate a vision of Chinese statehood that was rooted in the grandeur of its imperial past while being unequivocally geared towards a modern, inclusive future.

Chapter XVI: Integration and Transformation: Military, Governance, and Democratic Aspirations in the New Empire

The period following the reunification of the Nationalist government with mainland China, culminating in the restoration of the National Assembly and the re-establishment of a constitutional monarchy under the House of Zhu, marked a monumental phase in Chinese history. This chapter examines the complex process of institutional absorption and integration that facilitated the merging of disparate military and governance structures, bolstering the military-industrial capabilities of the new state while laying the groundwork for a nuanced approach to federalist governance and liberal democracy.

### Institutional Absorption and Integration

The reunification process involved the comprehensive amalgamation of existing institutional frameworks, with particular focus on legislative, military, and security apparatuses. This integration was pivotal in ensuring a seamless transition to the restored Empire's governance model while retaining the efficacy and capabilities of the existing structures.

#### Legislative Integration

The fusion of the Nationalist Chinese Legislative Yuan into the restored National Assembly marked a pivotal step towards creating a unified legislative body that bridged historical governance models with contemporary needs. This melding served several functions:

1. It preserved the legislative expertise and institutional memory of the Legislative Yuan, enhancing the effectiveness of the National Assembly.
2. It symbolized the reconciliation of Republic-era governance ideals with the monarchical framework, embodying a commitment to hybrid governance structures.
3. It facilitated legislative continuity, ensuring that laws and regulations would remain applicable and enforceable during the transitional phase.

#### Military Integration and Transformation

The absorption of the Peoples Liberation Army (PLA) into the Republic of China Armed Forces (ROCAF) and its subsequent transformation into a restored Beiyang Army/National Protection Army was a strategic endeavor aimed at consolidating military power while fostering a unified national defense identity. Simultaneously, the integration of the PLA Special Operations Forces (PLASOF) into the Airborne Special Service Company (ASSC) and the assimilation of the People's Armed Police (PAP) into the Thunder Squad underscored a comprehensive approach to enhancing special operations and rapid response capabilities.

This military integration achieved several objectives:

1. It streamlined command structures, eliminating redundancy and fostering efficacy in defense capabilities.
2. It retained the professional competencies and operational experiences of the PLA, enhancing the strategic and tactical proficiency of the new armed forces.
3. It symbolized the unification of previously competing military entities under a singular banner, promoting nationalistic pride and loyalty to the restored Empire.

#### Security Apparatus Consolidation

The integration of the People's Police into the Nationalist Chinese National Police Agency (NPA) represented a crucial step in consolidating internal security and law enforcement functions. This move rationalized policing efforts, creating a cohesive and responsive security mechanism capable of addressing the internal security requirements of the newly unified state.

### Military-Industrial Strengthening and Democratic Foundations

The reunification and the ensuing institutional integrations significantly enhanced the military and industrial strength of the new Empire, bolstering its capacity to embark on modernization initiatives. This period saw an emphasis on technological innovation, infrastructure development, and economic reform, aimed at revitalizing China's industrial base and projecting its military capabilities.

Concurrently, the reunification process was envisioned not merely as a structural reform but as an opportunity to lay the groundwork for a federalist, multi-party, liberal democracy within the unique framework of a constitutional monarchy. The integrated National Assembly became a platform for advocating and implementing democratic reforms, fostering political pluralism, and ensuring representation across China's diverse ethnic and regional constituencies.

The establishment of a federalist governance model underlined a commitment to accommodating the varied aspirations and identities within the vast Chinese state, offering a framework for shared governance and regional autonomy. Through deliberate constitutional provisions and political reforms, the new Empire sought to balance the monarchy's symbolic and unifying role with the principles of democratic participation and accountability.

### Conclusion

The integration of disparate military and governance structures into a coherent and unified framework under the restored Empire of China signified a crucial transitional phase towards realizing a modern state that revered its historical legacies while embracing the principles of democracy and federalism. These efforts reflected a profound commitment to forging a state that was militarily robust, industrially vibrant, and politically inclusive. The path towards establishing a federalist, multi-party democracy within a constitutional monarchy was fraught with challenges but underscored the transformative aspirations of a nation at a historical crossroads, seeking to redefine its identity and place in the modern world.
